does: This paper demonstrates that transformer-based prompt injection defenses achieve high classification accuracy yet fail to maintain geometric separation between clean and adversarially obfuscated prompts in their latent embedding spaces. The authors evaluate multiple BERT-family encoders against multi-operator obfuscation attacks combining homoglyphs, zero-width characters, and punctuation/emoji noise. They measure the phenomenon of latent embedding collapse by computing the minimal clean-obfuscated margin (delta = 1.02) and intra-class variance metrics (3.33 ± 6.23), revealing that obfuscated embeddings nearly overlap with clean ones despite near-perfect detection performance. This mismatch exposes a fundamental performance-robustness gap where standard classification metrics mask dangerous geometric fragility in the learned representations.
connects: This work extends prior research on adversarial robustness evaluation that moved beyond accuracy-only metrics to examine actual learned representations, building on findings that neural networks can maintain high performance while exhibiting brittle decision boundaries. It connects to the broader literature on prompt injection defenses and obfuscation attacks against language models, responding to the insufficiency of existing safety evaluations. The paper builds on geometric approaches to understanding neural network robustness, particularly work examining embedding space properties and margin-based measures of model confidence. It engages with the BERT-family encoders literature and their representational characteristics across varying depths and capacities, grounding the analysis in concrete architectural variation.
enables: These findings suggest new evaluation frameworks for prompt injection defenses that examine latent geometry alongside classification metrics, enabling more rigorous safety certification of language model defenses. The identified latent embedding collapse phenomenon points toward developing defense mechanisms that explicitly maintain geometric separation rather than optimizing only for classification accuracy. Future work can build on this geometric analysis to design obfuscation-robust embeddings or training objectives that penalize manifold overlap. The paper opens directions for understanding how depth and capacity affect representational robustness, potentially informing architecture choices for safer models. It also enables investigation of whether similar performance-robustness gaps exist in other safety-critical classification tasks, establishing a template for deeper evaluation of neural defenses.
